data.title: Wicked stars’ incredible costumes among exhibits at Oscar-winning designer’s new show
data.description:
Long before audiences saw Glinda’s dazzling pink dresses or Elphaba’s dramatic layered ensembles on the big screen, Paul Tazewell was imagining how silhouette, colour and texture would bring the witches of Oz to life.
Tazewell, the award-winning costume designer behind both Wicked films, the stage musical Hamilton and Steven Spielberg’s West Side Story, will be the focus of a new exhibition opening on January 19 at the Griffin Museum of Science and Industry in Chicago, Illinois.
“Crafting Character: The Costumes of Paul Tazewell” in the museum’s Kenneth C Griffin Studio will showcase designs all the way from early projects in Tazewell’s career to some of his most recognisable pieces in film, theatre, television and the red carpet.
“To allow visitors to engage directly with the work, to see it up close and in person, I’m hopeful that it will be exciting for them,” Tazewell says. “I think overall it would be a very full experience for anybody that chooses to come and see it.”

It is the designer’s debut exhibition and Tazewell says being approached by the museum was a blessing. His retrospective is near the museum’s 56th annual “Black Creativity Juried Art Exhibition”, which highlights black innovators and artists.
“I am so honoured that my exhibition sits adjacent to that as a celebration of the artist community here in Chicago,” he says.
Last year, Tazewell made history as the first black man to win the Academy Award for costume design, for his work on the first Wicked film – one of many milestones featured in the exhibition.
Visitors will follow a multimedia journey through Tazewell’s three-decade career and meticulous creative process. Each room will be accompanied by his audio narration, along with behind-the-scenes videos, original sketches and photographs.

The exhibition opens with a room centred on Tazewell’s inspirations, bringing together family photos, artwork by his mother and costume sketches from his college years.
Janelle Monáe’s viral 2025 Met Gala look, created by Tazewell in collaboration with designer Thom Browne, anchors the space, but a photo of Tazewell in high school marks the true beginning of his Ozian trajectory.
As a child growing up in Akron, Ohio, he was captivated by the shift from sepia-toned Kansas to vibrant Technicolor in the 1939 film The Wizard of Oz, a scene that still inspires him. At 16, he designed costumes for his school production of The Wiz. He would later win an Emmy in 2016 for costume design for The Wiz: Live! before moving on to the screen adaptation of the stage musical Wicked.
As the retrospective continues, viewers will see the intricate engineering behind Tazewell’s designs.
Anita’s yellow dress from West Side Story appears to ripple effortlessly as Ariana DeBose spins during the “America” sequence. Tazewell says numerous technical decisions went into achieving the effect.
“There’s geometry that goes into the pattern making. There are choices about fabrics that are specific to textile fibres and what those different aspects bring to manifesting costumes.”

Other signature pieces include costumes from Broadway’s Hamilton – from Thomas Jefferson’s purple suit to King George III’s royal ensemble and the Schuyler sisters’ Winter Ball gowns. Tazewell won his first Tony Award for the musical’s costume design, though he emphasises the contributions of the artisans who were on his team, such as tailor Artur Allakhverdyan.
In a room dedicated to collaboration, the exhibition spotlights the craftspeople behind Tazewell’s designs, including Miodrag Guberinic, who assisted him on the Wicked films, and Mark Zappone, who collaborated on The Sleeping Beauty ballet production for Pacific Northwest Ballet last year.
“There are many minds behind creating and realising a costume,” Tazewell says. “I’ve had the privilege to design alongside incredibly skilled makers.”
The final room of the exhibition is devoted to Tazewell’s work on Wicked. His most ambitious project to date, he designed more than 1,000 pieces for both films. In the first film, there were 25 costumes for Cynthia Erivo’s Elphaba and another 25 for Ariana Grande’s Glinda.

Tazewell wanted to capture the personalities of Elphaba and Glinda in what they wore, as he aims to do with all of his projects.
“There is an intention behind both conceptually what the costume is and how the character will be represented in the costume,” he says.
Glinda’s effervescent pink bubble dress is not only a nod to the original Wizard of Oz dress, but it is also meant to reflect an archetype of goodness. Made from layered nylon and silk organza, the dress comprises 137 pattern pieces and around 20,000 beads.
On the other hand, many of Elphaba’s dark ensembles, such as the one she wears while singing “Defying Gravity”, have mushroom-inspired micro pleats and layered textures that reflect her affinity to nature and animals.
For Tazewell, the exhibition is about more than seeing his designs up close.
“I hope it inspires young people to want to be a costume designer, and at the very least, that it inspires creativity,” he says. “To be that kind of inspiration is really, I think, a big part of why I’m here, why I’m here on Earth.”

data.title: 73% of Hong Kong, Greater Bay Area firms speeding up expansion into Asean: survey
data.description:
Nearly three-quarters of enterprises from Hong Kong and Greater Bay Area cities will speed up their expansion into the Asean market as a primary strategy to mitigate risks from international trade frictions and shifting global supply chains, according to a survey.
The survey and research findings, co-published by the Hong Kong Trade Development Council and Singapore-based United Overseas Bank on Wednesday, also revealed that about 98 per cent of enterprises are planning to keep or expand their operations in the Southeast Asian region.
The survey, carried out from July to August 2025, polled 600 companies based in Hong Kong and five key cities in the bay area: Guangzhou, Shenzhen, Foshan, Dongguan and Zhongshan.
“With the shifting global supply chain landscape and tariff uncertainties, many Greater Bay Area enterprises are accelerating their Asean engagement not merely as a means of risk diversification, but to capture new growth opportunities in fast-evolving markets,” Chu Wing-chor, the council’s deputy director of research, said.
“Hong Kong’s unique positioning as a superconnector allows businesses to leverage the city’s comprehensive trade, financial and professional services platform to navigate this transformation efficiently.”
According to the survey findings, 73 per cent of bay area respondents planned to accelerate their business expansion efforts in Asean countries, underlining the region’s significance as a pivotal and increasingly pressing growth driver amid global trade tensions.
The research highlighted a significant 25 percentage point rise from the previous year in the number of bay area firms looking to either expand or maintain their sales activities within the Asean, with 98 per cent of the companies surveyed still focusing on these vibrant sales markets.
Asean economies were progressively gaining importance, the study found, with Singapore, Vietnam, Thailand, Malaysia and Indonesia emerging as primary sites for bay area enterprises in the next three years.
To facilitate their expansion strategies, businesses anticipated dedicating an average of 30 per cent more resources towards their Asean growth initiatives.
Vietnam stood out as the top priority for increased investment, with companies aiming for a 47 per cent boost in resources, followed by Indonesia at 37 per cent, and Thailand and Malaysia each at 32 per cent.

The overarching strategic focus revolved around bolstering sales growth, particularly in Thailand, Vietnam and Indonesia, while broadening firms’ production and sourcing hubs, with Vietnam, Thailand and Malaysia being the preferred locations.
Despite Singapore’s already strong presence among enterprises in bay area cities, respondents said they would still aim to increase investments by an average of 23 per cent to speed up business expansion, notably in financing and regional office set-ups, in the city state.
Gary Ng Cheuk-yan, a senior economist at Natixis Corporate and Investment Bank, said that while no country could fully hide from protectionism and geopolitical tensions, Asean was the most feasible shelter from the US-China trade war.
“For Greater Bay Area firms, Asean offers the advantages of rising potential growth, close geographic proximity and access to the Chinese diaspora, which may make it easier to form partnerships,” he said.
“However, they should be wary of risks posed by government red tape, regulatory fragmentation and complexity, local competition, cultural differences, currency volatility and underdeveloped infrastructure.”
Danny Lau Tat-pong, honorary chairman of the Hong Kong Small and Medium Enterprises Association and managing director of Kam Pin Industrial, a Hong Kong-based architectural metal fabricator known for its aluminium products, held a more cautious view on the feasibility of replacing Western demand with Southeast Asian consumers.
“Asean countries have their own production. There are some products they don’t produce themselves, or they do but the quality is not that good; in that case, your chances might be stronger. But if your price is not low and your quality is about the same, your competitiveness will be very weak,” he said.
“To develop a new market, you have to mobilise a lot of resources, including money and manpower before you can develop the local market with a lot of promotional efforts.”
Lau also highlighted a stark disparity in potential trade volume, warning that even if a business successfully broke into the Asean market, it may find sales volume to be much lower than in the US.

data.title: China’s anti-corruption watchdog reveals how former banker fell in bribery scandal
data.description:
China’s anti-corruption watchdog has detailed how disgraced former banker and Communist Party chief of Hubei province Jiang Chaoliang received bribes for favours, ultimately landing himself and his brothers under investigation.
The revelations came in an episode of a CCTV documentary series that aired on Tuesday night during the fifth plenary session of the Central Commission for Discipline Inspection (CCDI).
Held at the beginning of each year, the session aims to sum up the achievements of President Xi Jinping’s sweeping anti-corruption campaign and lay out areas to focus on in the new year.
On Wednesday, the closing communique of the plenary session called for maintaining “high-pressure deterrence” against corruption.
It said that “new and hidden forms of corruption” would be targeted.
It also pledged that anti-corruption authorities would “intensify efforts to investigate cross-border corruption cases” and cooperate with the top legislature in formulating a law against cross-border corruption.
Jiang, 68, was sent to the southern province of Guangdong during the 1997 Asian financial crisis. While there, he worked alongside then vice-governor Wang Qishan to manage the fallout. Wang would go on to become the future vice-president of China.
Jiang later occupied several high-ranking positions within the central bank and the financial industry, including chairman and party secretary of the Bank of Communications, vice-chairman and president of China Development Bank, and chairman and party secretary of the Agricultural Bank of China (ABC).


According to the CCTV documentary, businessman Li Yuanguang spent over a decade cultivating close ties with Jiang’s family, starting from when Jiang was a mid-level ABC executive.
Li paid for Jiang’s children’s education and medical care for his parents. He even gave money to Jiang’s mother for what Li called “mahjong funds” and financed a property purchase for the family’s nanny.
The businessman’s long game paid off when Jiang, who became ABC chairman in 2011, helped Li’s company secure a massive automated teller machine (ATM) procurement contract.
Central to the corruption scheme were Jiang’s two younger brothers: Jiang Binliang and Jiang Zhongliang.
They leveraged their brother’s influence, according to the report, with Jiang Zhongliang admitting to acting as a matchmaker for deals, while Jiang Binliang enjoyed a lavish lifestyle funded by the graft.
Neither held official positions but both became conduits for bribes, as businessmen funnelled millions of yuan through them for projects and approvals.
Li even invited Jiang’s brothers to “invest” in his company at no cost, registering them as shareholders without requiring them to provide funds. In return, Jiang Chaoliang helped Li secure contracts and other benefits.
Jiang Chaoliang was appointed governor of the northeastern province of Jilin in 2014 and was promoted to party chief of central Hubei province two years later.
He remained as Hubei party chief until February 2020, when he was removed as Beijing authorities responded to public anger over what was seen as the botched handling of the Covid-19 pandemic outbreak, which began in Wuhan, the provincial capital.
The Jiang family story ended with Jiang Chaoliang’s corruption investigation in February of last year and subsequent expulsion from the party and public office.
His suspected crimes were referred to prosecutors for investigation, and those who helped him or gave him bribes have faced consequences, according to the documentary. But it did not reveal whether Jiang’s brothers had been punished yet.
Jiang is among the most prominent figures caught in the party’s decade-long anti-corruption campaign, which snared a record 65 high-ranking officials last year.
Those detained included top provincial and city officials, ministry leaders, financial regulators, bankers, state enterprise executives, university presidents and military top brass.
The CCDI plenum communique said China’s anti-corruption investigators would continue to crack down on misconduct in sectors including finance, state-owned enterprises, energy, education, academic societies, development zones and public procurement.
The key sectors last year were finance, state-owned enterprises, energy, fire safety, tobacco, pharmaceuticals, universities, sports, public welfare and charity, construction projects and bidding processes.
A Wednesday commentary by party mouthpiece People’s Daily called for “putting power in the cage in a more scientific way”, citing a recent speech by Xi.
It added that the party would amend existing discipline rules to be more specific and feasible. It also said the party would step up efforts to make the rules equally binding for all “with no exception”.

data.title: Li Ka-shing’s CK Infrastructure gets nod to sell train leasing firm UK Rails
data.description:
CK Infrastructure Holdings is poised to complete the disposal of UK Rails (Eversholt Rail) this month following London’s approval of the deal, according to the company, which is part of Hong Kong tycoon Li Ka-shing’s business empire.
The train leasing company is owned by a consortium of the group’s companies: CK Infrastructure, which holds 65 per cent, along with CK Asset Holdings (20 per cent), Power Asset Holdings (10 per cent) and CK Hutchison Holdings (5 per cent), according to the group.
The deal, which had secured the nod of the UK’s Competition and Markets Authority, would “generate substantial revenue”, CK Infrastructure said on Wednesday.
The price was not disclosed, but earlier reports suggested that the group was seeking a valuation of as much as £4 billion (US$5.38 billion). The group acquired the asset in 2015 for £2.5 billion.
During CK Holdings’ interim results announcement in August, chairman Victor Li Tzar-kuoi said that the decision to sell the group’s interest in UK Rails would allow the “joint venture to unlock the potential value of the asset”.
“Only by buying and selling can a business grow bigger,” Li said. “We have never liked borrowing, so we reserve funds from time to time for larger transactions.”
Eversholt Rail is a top UK rail vehicle leasing company. It serves rail and freight operators by leasing them regional, short-distance and high-speed passenger trains, as well as freight locomotives, under long-term contracts.
The sale came after CK Infrastructure agreed in August to pay around £350 million for 32 wind farms in the UK.
In April, it formed a consortium with CK Asset and Power Assets to acquire Lionrai Investments No 1, which owns 100 per cent of Northern Ireland natural gas network Phoenix Energy from NatWest Group Pension Fund and Utilities Trust of Australia for £757 million.
Phoenix Energy is one of three gas distribution network operators in Northern Ireland. Its network covers nearly half of the local population, including Greater Belfast, and accounts for 78 per cent of gas connections in Northern Ireland.

Two weeks later in May, CK bought UU Solar, which owns and operates 70 renewable power-generation assets in the UK, for £90.8 million.
In March last year, CK Hutchison, one of the flagship companies of the group, announced its bid to sell an 80 per cent stake in its overseas port assets to a consortium led by US firm BlackRock and Terminal Investment, the terminal arm of the world’s largest container shipper, Mediterranean Shipping.
The deal, valued at about US$23 billion, would encompass more than 40 terminal ports across more than 20 countries.
The transaction, however, has yet to be completed following Beijing’s opposition to the sale amid trade disputes with Washington.

data.title: With India’s Super League starting next month, will they be ready for Hong Kong in March?
data.description:
The delayed Indian Super League will kick off on February 14 after all 14 football clubs confirmed participation, officials said Wednesday.
The ISL, India’s flagship football competition, is usually played between September and April.
It faced uncertainty because a commercial rights agreement between the AIFF and Football Sports Development Limited, which operated the ISL, expired on December 9, leaving the league without a sponsor.
Some clubs, including reigning champions Mohun Bagan, halted player salaries and suspended football activities in December.
The All India Football Federation (AIFF) plans to take over the running of the ISL from the 2025-26 season under a 20-year proposal sent to clubs, according to local media reports.
“Many clubs had agreed earlier, but the remaining few came on board on Tuesday,” an AIFF source told AFP on condition of anonymity.

Talks are continuing to finalise a home-and-away format for a 91-match season after Sports Minister Mansukh Mandaviya announced last week that all 14 clubs would compete.
The club football chaos has been replicated at national-team level. A last-ditch 1-0 Asian Cup qualifier loss in Hong Kong last June proved the end for head coach Manolo Marquez following an unhappy year in charge. The Spaniard is still boss of ISL club FC Goa, which he joined in June 2023.
Indian football has long struggled to find its footing on the global stage despite a passionate fan base and a rich history, and the national team has continued to flatline under current manager Khalid Jamil.
They are bottom of their Asian qualifying group after collecting two points and scoring only two goals from five matches. The world’s No 124-ranked team when Marquez replaced Igor Stimac, India are now languishing at No 142.
Their final qualifying game, against Hong Kong, on March 31, is a dead rubber, given both sides’ hopes of reaching the 2027 Saudi Arabian finals have disappeared, but it has the capacity to shape the city team’s immediate future.
The game will provide Roberto Losada, Hong Kong’s interim head coach, a first genuine chance to press his claims for the permanent role. After replacing Ashley Westwood, who left after Hong Kong’s Asian Cup elimination, Losada oversaw the recent two-legged Guangdong-Hong Kong Cup, won by the mainland Chinese side on penalties.
His next assignment is likely to be a friendly in the days before playing India, but the 49-year-old will finally get his competitive international baptism in South Asia. The visit to India could also provide an opportunity to inject fresh blood into the team, as Hong Kong tentatively turn their attention towards reaching the next Asian finals in 2031.
Of the side that started the defeat by Singapore in November that extinguished Hong Kong’s finals hopes, five players are 30 or older.
Launched in 2014 with global stars such as Italy’s Alessandro Del Piero, the franchise-based ISL aimed to give Indian football a glamorous reboot.
But television ratings have slumped and sponsor interest has waned.
The AIFF invited bids for a new commercial partner under the supervision of a Supreme Court-appointed committee but failed to find any takers.

data.title: Singapore’s Pritam Singh debate: MPs trade barbs over bid to remove opposition leader
data.description:
Singapore’s parliamentarians on Wednesday debated Workers’ Party chief Pritam Singh’s suitability as Leader of the Opposition following his court conviction for lying to a committee, with his opponents questioning his integrity and leadership, while supporters suggested there was a political agenda against him.
The discussion, which is ongoing and has extended into the evening, centred on Leader of the House Indranee Rajah’s motion calling on parliament to consider that Singh’s “conviction and conduct render him unsuitable to continue as the Leader of the Opposition” and to express regret at his conduct, which was “dishonourable and unbecoming” of an MP.
Singh was the first to be appointed to the official role in 2020 by then prime minister Lee Hsien Loong after the general election that year, when the WP won a record 10 out of 93 seats.
On Wednesday, Indranee clarified that the motion asked parliamentarians, predominantly People’s Action Party (PAP) members, to express a view of Singh’s conduct, and if the House were to agree that how he carried himself was not acceptable, they should express regret.
Indranee also noted that the decision on whether Singh should continue with that designation rested with Prime Minister Lawrence Wong, not parliament, but MPs should weigh in on Singh’s suitability. It will then be for Wong to decide the next step.
“This is a serious matter. The misconduct in question is not trivial. It involves multiple lies to different persons at different times, guiding a junior MP to do the wrong thing and a failure of leadership,” Indranee said.
She stressed that in Singapore’s parliamentary democracy, honesty and integrity were neither abstract ideals nor optional virtues, but were the foundations of public trust.

“The authority of this House does not rest on law alone. It rests on the confidence and trust that Singaporeans have in parliament, in its processes and its members. This confidence and trust can be lost. This is why parliament must insist that its members always speak truthfully and act with integrity, even when it is politically inconvenient to do so,” Indranee said.
Singh, MP for Aljunied constituency, was fined S$14,000 (US$10,880) for lying under oath to a parliamentary committee looking into the lie former WP MP Raeesah Khan told in parliament. His conviction was upheld on December 4 after he appealed it.
On Wednesday, Indranee said: “When the office of the Leader of the Opposition was first introduced, there were high hopes for the role and how it would enhance the functioning and standing of parliament. But now, Mr Singh has done all these things that I have set out and been convicted in court for lying to a committee of parliament under these circumstances, the conclusion is clear and beyond doubt.
“Mr Singh’s conduct was dishonourable and unbecoming of an MP, and he has fallen short of the requirements and standards expected of a leader of the opposition.”
She urged members to vote in favour of the motion if they agreed, and if they disagreed, they should justify to the House and to Singaporeans “why Singapore should accept such lower standards of honesty and integrity from their political leaders”.
Singh, who has led the WP since 2018, lifted the party whip to allow the 12 WP MPs in the House to vote as they wished.
He disagreed with the courts’ judgment and the assertion that his conduct was dishonourable and unbecoming of an MP.

Singh cited President Tharman Shanmugaratnam as an example of someone who maintained his innocence even after a court ruling against him. In 2023, just before the presidential election that he won, Tharman told local media outlet Mothership that he believed prosecution had got it wrong in his 1992 conviction of revealing official secrets.
“A criminal conviction does not negate one’s right to assert innocence; a finding of guilt by the court did not undermine the president’s personal view of his conviction. In my case, my conscience will always be clear insofar as my conviction on both charges is concerned,” Singh said.
Yet he reiterated that he took full responsibility for not responding quickly enough to correct Khan’s lie in her speech.
“The Leader of the Opposition appointment is not a constitutional or statutory one. I have never operated on the assumption that the Leader of the Opposition appointment is a given, nor have I hankered for it. I have done my best to work with my colleagues to advance the interests of Singaporeans with the Workers’ Party as a responsible opposition through the office,” Singh said.
WP MP Sylvia Lim noted that Singh’s case was different from other situations where MPs had admitted to their wrongdoing, as Singh had maintained his innocence. “The question that arises is whether parliament should subject Mr Singh to a further punishment by initiating a removal of him from his position as the leader of the opposition. Is this necessary, or is this a political exercise?” Lim questioned.
“I urged that all of us in this House see each other as fellow Singaporeans who are not enemies or competitors, who have common cause in serving fellow citizens through this House. I emphasise our duty to ensure that this House serves the interests of citizens outside the House … I believe this motion today does not serve Singaporeans, but is a party political exercise,” Lim said.
Part of the motion involved noting that the High Court judgment and the parliamentary findings had implications for Lim and former WP MP Muhamad Faisal Abdul Manap, which have to be considered separately.

Lim said that there were several references to her in the judgment but these were based on the prosecution’s evidence, and noted that she and Faisal were not called as witnesses and had no opportunity to present their side of the story to the court. “The court’s findings cannot be held against us. The leader, a senior counsel with decades of litigation experience, will no doubt be aware of what I had just said,” Lim said.
Three nominated members of parliament, who are meant to be non-partisan voices in parliament, have expressed support for the motion.
Senior Parliamentary Secretary Syed Harun Alhabsyi, a former nominated MP, said that to lie and cover it up was against the trust given to MPs by the public and urged against shirking from responsibility to correct this behaviour and dishonesty, and that MPs should uphold integrity and honour.
Lim questioned Syed Harun’s “pontificating about integrity and institutions”, noting that he had given up his nominated MP seat prematurely to run in the 2025 general election for the PAP.
Speaker Seah Kian Peng allowed Syed Harun to respond despite noting that Lim’s point was not relevant to the debate.
Syed Harun noted that the issue was on public record and that when he was a nominated member of parliament, he was very clearly non-partisan. “I’m quite clear in terms of my thoughts about integrity,” the PAP MP said.
As Leader of the Opposition, Singh’s privileges include an annual allowance of $385,000, double that of an elected MP, and an additional allowance to hire up to three additional legislative assistants. He is also provided with an office and a secretary.

In parliament, he is generally given the right of first response among MPs and gets more time for his speeches.
According to a ministerial statement on the duties and privileges of the Leader of the Opposition in 2020, Singh also receives confidential government briefings on select matters of national security and external relations, and will be briefed in the event of a national crisis or emergency.
A WP spokesman said on Friday the party would address the matter during the parliamentary sitting, where the motion would be debated, and it did not have further comments to make at the time.
On January 3, the party said its central executive committee directed a disciplinary panel to be formed to determine if Singh contravened the party’s constitution. There was also a request by cadres for a special conference to be held to discuss the issue, which will be called after the disciplinary panel has completed its work.
The party said that the disciplinary process would be concluded within three months and notice of the special conference would be issued within two weeks after the conclusion of the disciplinary process.

data.title: Animal welfare groups slam Hong Kong pet shop for offering cat rental service
data.description:
Animal welfare groups have criticised a Hong Kong pet shop for offering a cat rental service, calling the move unethical and harmful to the felines’ well-being.
The Agriculture, Fisheries and Conservation Department (AFCD) said on Wednesday that it had sent staff to inspect the shop in North Point and reminded its operator to comply with the law.
In a social media post – which appears to have been since removed – the Petbook pet shop advertised a “cat friend rental” service. It offered people the chance to rent a cat for a week for HK$800 with a refundable deposit of HK$6,500.
After the one-week trial period ended, clients could either buy the cat at no extra cost – meaning the price was the initial HK$7,300 handed over – or return it for a refund of their deposit.
The post said the service included items for the cat, as well as one week’s worth of pet food. The store would also provide a one-on-one coaching session on how to help the cat adapt to new environments.
The shop also said in its promotional materials that it underwent regular inspections by the department and that its business was “legal and transparent”.
The Post visited the shop on Wednesday but no one answered the door. The shop was also approached for comment.

The Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals (SPCA), responding to queries from the Post, said it “strongly opposes” the concept of pet rental services, adding that while the practice was “uncommon”, it harmed animal welfare for several reasons.
The charity called the practice unethical as it commodified sentient beings. It said cats were also highly sensitive to their environment and frequent changes could lead to stress that affected their physical and psychological health.
A spokesman also said that it was unclear what would happen to the cats if they were rented multiple times without finding a permanent home, which could mean abandonment or exploitation for breeding.
It was also unclear whether the shop had a screening mechanism to ensure the pets received proper care.
“Pets are sentient beings; they deserve to be treated with respect and to receive proper care in a safe environment,” the spokesman said.
“We urge the public to understand their responsibility and to carefully assess their capacity to care for an animal before jumping into pet ownership.”
Lau Chun-hoi, coordinator of the NGO Animal Welfare Advocacy Alliance, said the group objected to the practice of pet rentals.
Beyond the negative effects on the well-being of the animals, Lau said such services could encourage people to think that getting a pet could have a “trial” first.
He added that while education could bring changes to how society viewed getting pets, the best way to prevent the issue from happening again was to amend animal welfare laws to include “duty of care” provisions.
“In our developed society nowadays, it is not just about providing these animals with food and water. You need to give them an appropriate environment, you need to give them a way for them to be their natural selves, you need to take them to a veterinarian when they are sick.”
The department said that its Code of Practice for Animal Traders stated that merchants had a duty of care to their animals at all times.
It added that the pet shop held a valid licence.
“The animal trader involved has a valid Animal Trader Licence, the department has already reminded the licence holder to strictly follow the requirements stipulated by the relevant laws,” a spokesman said.
“The department has already arranged staff to inspect the relevant store and closely monitor their operation. If it finds any behaviour that does not adhere to regulations or licensing conditions, it will take further action.”
The department also reminded residents to be responsible pet owners, adding that they should think carefully before getting a pet.

data.title: How Putin’s focus on Ukraine war leaves allies like Venezuela, Iran and Cuba exposed
data.description:
As Russian President Vladimir Putin focuses his attentions on Moscow’s war in Ukraine, his strategic allies around the world have been left feeling neglected – or worse.
In Venezuela, officials now think their years-long security relationship with Moscow was a paper tiger.
They’re not alone: From Damascus and Tehran to Havana, over the last 13 months authoritarian regimes which previously benefited from their close ties to the Kremlin have found Russian support lacking when it mattered most.
Syrian dictator Bashar al-Assad fled to Moscow after Russian military backing withered away.

Cuba, lacking a benefactor, is confronting a humanitarian crisis some think could see it become the next domino to fall. Iran was bombed by the US last year and now Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei faces domestic protests on an existential scale, along with the threat of more US military action.
The most potent symbol of Russia’s inattention may be that of Venezuela’s Nicolas Maduro languishing in a New York jail. As the remnants of the state security apparatus conduct a postmortem on how their leader was captured by the US, officials there privately expressed dismay at the failure of their Cuban and Russian partners to help protect him, according to people familiar with the matter.
Despite repeated public signals from US President Donald Trump that he intended to remove Maduro, officials in Caracas complain that the Cuban and Russian intelligence agencies on which they relied for much of their security failed to identify vulnerabilities or any specific information about the threat to the Venezuelan leader.

Much of his personal security was in the hands of Cuban intelligence officials who acted as his personal protection team, a factor illustrated by the Cuban government announcing the deaths of 32 of its nationals during the US operation.
There is no trust left between Venezuela and its Cuban security counterparts, the officials said.
They also cite the failings of Russian S-300 and Buk-M2 air defence systems in protecting Venezuela’s airspace. Russia did not give adequate technical support to ensure the systems were operationally effective, the officials have complained. Venezuela’s cyber defences were reliant on technical support from Russia, they said, which again was found wanting as US cyberattacks appeared to cut off power to large areas of Caracas.

The consequence is a breakdown in trust in the security partnership between Venezuela, Cuba and Russia. In Caracas, Maduro’s successor Delcy Rodriguez now has little option but to accept American offers of cooperation and to loosen ties with her country’s old partners.
Putin has not yet publicly commented on the US actions in Venezuela, with the Russian Foreign Ministry issuing a fairly formal statement highlighting that such actions violate key principles of international law. However, Russian officials were angered by Trump proceeding with the operation to capture Maduro, according to people familiar with the matter.
Still, relations with the US are more important to Moscow now than Venezuela, a separate person familiar with the Kremlin’s position said, declining to be identified because the information is not public.
Maduro’s capture is unpleasant for Russia, but not a catastrophe, the person said. Iran, by contrast, is a more serious issue, as Russian cooperation with the country is much stronger, including in the military field, they said.

Russia may publicly support Iran, but is unlikely to become deeply involved in helping Tehran, given its limited capacity and the priority of ending the war with Ukraine, the person said.
That bodes ill for Russia’s other strategic partnerships, and suggests that it’s all the more important to the Kremlin that it achieves all its aims in Ukraine without concessions at the negotiating table. That in turn may make it harder still for Trump to secure an elusive peace deal.

data.title: Indonesian comic hit with blasphemy complaints over Netflix jokes
data.description:
A police complaint against one of Indonesia’s best-known comedians has reignited debate over satire, blasphemy and free expression in the world’s third-largest democracy.
The case involves 46-year-old stand-up comic and actor Pandji Pragiwaksono, whose Netflix special Mens Rea – an unfiltered performance touching on politics, law and democracy – was recorded in Jakarta before an audience of around 10,000 people on December 30.
Pandji’s routine pokes fun at Indonesia’s political elite, taking aim at President Prabowo Subianto, Vice-President Gibran Rakabuming Raka, former leader Joko Widodo and the national police.
However, it was his jokes about Nahdlatul Ulama (NU) and Muhammadiyah, the country’s two largest Muslim organisations, that triggered the backlash.

On January 8, groups claiming to represent the youth wings of NU and Muhammadiyah filed police complaints accusing Pandji of public incitement and blasphemy.
“We reported [Pandji] as we felt he was demeaning, slanderous and prone to cause a commotion,” Rizki Abdul Rahman Wahid of NU Youth Force was cited as saying by CNN Indonesia, alleging that the comic had suggested the two groups received mining concessions in return for political support.
Both NU and Muhammadiyah have since distanced themselves from the complaints, saying the youth groups are not officially affiliated.
Ulil Abshar Abdalla, head of NU’s executive board, said he regretted the move. “We need a lot of laughter in this country,” he added. “It would be a shame if a comedian who makes so many people laugh had to be reported to the authorities.”
We need a lot of laughter in this country
Ulil Abshar Abdalla, Nahdlatul Ulama official
Pandji has also received support online, with film director Indra Yudhistira writing on social media that Mens Rea marked “a shift in the function of stand-up” in Indonesia, from entertainment to “social corrective”.
He praised Pandji for depicting “the sociopolitical anxieties of Indonesia with a precision that mainstream journalism might not dare to touch”.
Responding to the complaints, Pandji said he respected his critics’ right to report him.
“If I want to be given the freedom to say whatever I want, then others also have the freedom to say whatever they want,” he told the Tempo news outlet on January 9, adding that he was open to explaining his material to them – and he hoped that they would laugh.
Separate reports have also been filed against Pandji in Malang, East Java, by two other groups alleging blasphemy and insults to Islam. One group objected to his suggestion that voters should consider candidates’ qualifications rather than their religiosity.
‘Not a crime’
Legal experts suggest that the complaints may struggle to meet the threshold for prosecution under Indonesia’s 2023 Criminal Code.
The Institute for Criminal Justice Reform said Pandji’s remarks “did not constitute a criminal offence”, noting that incitement required actively urging someone to commit a crime – something his jokes clearly did not do.
Criminal law scholar Hery Firmansyah of Tarumanagara University said investigators must determine whether Pandji’s material “really contains elements of hostility”.
He added that the comedian could bring expert witnesses to show that his jokes fell under artistic expression and did “not fulfil the elements of the crime”.

Police in Jakarta have pledged to consult legal experts, with Director of General Criminal Investigation Iman Imanuddin vowing in a statement on Monday “to maintain professionalism” and “protect freedom of expression in art”.
Nur Ansar, a researcher with the Institute for Criminal Justice Reform, called the blasphemy complaint “irrelevant”, arguing that the material neither insulted any religion nor spread hatred.
“NU and Muhammadiyah have been given mining concessions since 2024. It’s been widely reported, so the matter is clear,” he said. “None of the articles used by the complainant is relevant.”

Silencing satire?
Hery said that under Indonesia’s revised criminal code, authorities could pursue “restorative justice” against Pandji instead of prosecution.
“If Pandji is prosecuted, many people would be caught up in the same case,” he said. “This sets a bad precedent for those who express their grievances or criticism through satire.”
The Jakarta Legal Aid Institute voiced similar concerns, saying the complaints risked creating “a chilling effect” on artists and critics.
“The phenomenon of ‘self-censorship’ will occur, meaning that people independently censor themselves to avoid criticising or expressing anything that is disapproved of by those in power,” it said in a statement on January 9.
“This will undoubtedly have negative consequences for democracy, the rule of law and human rights.”

data.title: Taiwan names senior DPP politician new cross-strait chief amid Beijing’s scepticism
data.description:
Taiwan has appointed a senior Democratic Progressive Party (DPP) politician and former liaison to Japan to head the semi-official group that manages cross-strait ties in a move Beijing has met with scepticism.
Su Jia-chyuan, former head of the Taiwan-Japan Relations Association under Taipei’s foreign ministry, will chair the Straits Exchange Foundation (SEF), according to Karen Kuo, spokeswoman for the office of Taiwan’s leader William Lai Ching-te, on Tuesday.
The SEF is managed by Taipei’s Mainland Affairs Council and handles technical and administrative cross-strait affairs. Its mainland counterpart is the Association for Relations Across the Taiwan Straits (Arats), administered by the Taiwan Affairs Office (TAO).
The SEF and Arats had played significant roles in political communication between Taipei and Beijing, underpinned by senior leadership support on both sides.
Su, 69, is a senior politician from the ruling independence-leaning DPP. He stood as former Taiwanese leader Tsai Ing-wen’s running mate in the island’s 2012 election, though the pair lost to the now-opposition Kuomintang party, led by Ma Ying-jeou.

Su served as speaker of the Legislative Yuan during Tsai’s first term from 2016 to 2020. He briefly held the post of secretary general of her office during her second term.
From May 2022 until last week, Su headed the Taiwan-Japan Relations Association, overseeing bilateral ties prioritised by the DPP administration.
In Tuesday’s announcement, Kuo described Su as having “laid a solid foundation for Taiwan-Japan friendship”, adding that Lai expected him to “facilitate healthy, positive exchanges and development across the strait”.
The Mainland Affairs Council on Tuesday said it would continue to follow Lai’s cross-strait policy and “Taiwan’s mainstream public opinion”.
The council would be “jointly striving to maintain the status quo of peace and stability across the Taiwan Strait and prudently promoting cross-strait exchanges that are reciprocal, dignified, healthy and orderly”, it added.
Established in 1991 after Taiwan lifted its decades-long ban on mainland family visits, the SEF is best known for facilitating the historic 1993 Singapore summit between its late chairman, Koo Chen-fu, and his mainland counterpart, Wang Daohan – the first public meeting between official representatives from both sides since 1949.
Representatives of the SEF and Arats also fostered the “1992 Consensus”, an oral agreement reached more than three decades ago affirming there is only one China, but that each side may have its own interpretation of what that means.
Beijing argues this consensus constitutes recognition of the one-China principle and insists that its acceptance is a prerequisite for cross-strait exchanges.


Since the DPP assumed office in 2016, Beijing has repeatedly said that Taipei’s refusal to accept the consensus has undermined cross-strait exchanges, citing this as grounds for suspending communication between Arats and SEF that year.
The communication mechanism remains suspended to this day.
The SEF’s former chairman, Wu Feng-shan, resigned last month after little more than a year at the helm.
According to Taiwanese media, Wu stated that since 2016, Arats had “read but not responded” to more than 10,000 letters sent by the foundation.
In Beijing on Wednesday, TAO spokeswoman Zhu Fenglian said dialogue mechanisms between Arats and the SEF could only resume if the DPP authorities acknowledged the “1992 Consensus embodying the one-China principle”, adding that “the SEF could then fulfil its proper functions”.
Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China to be reunited by force if necessary. Most countries, including the United States and Japan, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state, but Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the self-ruled island by force and is committed to supplying it with weapons.
Cross-strait civilian exchanges have steadily eroded since 2016, a decline that accelerated following Lai’s 2024 inauguration. Beijing has called the Taiwanese leader a “troublemaker” and “separatist”.
In recent years, Beijing has conducted increasingly sophisticated military drills around the island, including blockade simulations.
Beijing has also condemned Lai’s deepening ties with Tokyo, accusing his administration of “whitewashing” Japan’s colonial history.
